turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The second case is Kokesh versus Securities and Exchange Commission. The Securities and Exchange Commission possesses authority to investigate violations of federal securities laws and to commence enforcement actions in federal district courts for any such violations. Congress has granted the Commission authority to seek monetary civil penalties in enforcement actions. And since the 1970s, the Commission has also sought disgorgement of the wrongdoer's gains from illegal activities. This Court has held that 28 U.S.C. §2462, which establishes a five-year limitations period for any action, suit, or proceeding for the enforcement of any civil fine, penalty, or forfeiture applies when the Commission seeks monetary civil penalties. This case presents the question whether §2462's limitation period also applies when the Commission seeks disgorgement. We hold it does. In 2009, the Commission brought in an enforcement action alleging the petitioner Charles Kokesh violated various securities laws by concealing the misappropriation of about $34.9 million from four business development companies over a nearly 10-year period. After a jury found that Kokesh's actions violated several securities laws, the district court determined that §2462's five-years limitation period applied to the government's request for monetary civil penalties. With respect to the – the requested $34.9 million in disgorgement, however, the Court concluded that §2462 did not apply because disgorgement is not a penalty within the meaning of the statute. The Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit Affirmed, holding that disgorgement was neither a penalty or a forfeiture. We grant this certiorari and now reverse. The statute of limitations set forth in §2462 applies here if disgorgement is either a fine, penalty, or forfeiture. This Court has explained that a penalty is a punishment, whether corporal or punitary, imposed and enforced by the state for a crime or offense against its laws. This definition gives rise to two principles. First, whether a sanction represents a penalty, turns in part on whether the wrong sought to be redressed is a wrong to the public or a wrong to an individual. Second, a sanction operates as a penalty if it is sought for the purpose of punishment and to deter rather than to compensate victims. The applications of these principles here readily demonstrate that as SS -- SEC disgorgement constitutes a penalty within the meaning of §2462. First, SEC disgorgement is imposed by the courts as a consequence for violating public laws. That is, for a violation committed against the United States rather than an aggrieved individual. Second, SEC disgorgement is imposed to deter violations of the securities laws. Sanctions imposed for the purpose of deterring infractions of public laws are inherently punitive because deterrence is not a legitimate non-punitive governmental objective -- objective. Finally, SEC disgorgement is often not compensatory. Disgorged profits are paid to the district courts which have discretion to determine how the money will be distributed. They may distribute the funds to victims, but no statute commands them to do so. And courts often distribute such funds to the U.S. Treasury. Because disgorgement requires that defendants pay a non-compensatory sanction to the government as a consequence of a legal violation, it is a penalty under §2462. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit. The decision of the Court is unanimous.
